subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Counsel is drafting a statement of claim on behalf of a client who suffered injuries in a motor vehicle accident. Counsel wants to ensure the pleading meets all requirements under the Rules of Civil Procedure.
call_of_question: Under r.25.06 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following is a requirement for a statement of claim?

options.A: The statement of claim must contain the evidence that the plaintiff intends to rely upon at trial.
options.B: The statement of claim must contain a concise statement of the material facts on which the party relies for the claim, but not the evidence by which those facts are to be proved.
options.C: The statement of claim must include a summary of all documentary evidence the plaintiff intends to produce.
options.D: The statement of claim must be accompanied by affidavit evidence sworn by the plaintiff confirming the facts alleged.

explanation: Under r.25.06(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), every pleading shall contain a concise statement of the material facts on which the party relies for the claim or defence, but not the evidence by which those facts are to be proved. This is the fundamental distinction between pleadings and evidence: a statement of claim states what happened (material facts), not how those facts will be proven (evidence). This rule applies equally to all pleadings in Ontario civil proceedings.
why_A_wrong: A statement of claim must not contain the evidence by which facts are to be proved. Rule 25.06(1) expressly distinguishes between material facts (which must be pleaded) and evidence (which must not be pleaded).
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 25.06(1) requires a concise statement of material facts but expressly excludes the evidence by which those facts are to be proved.
why_C_wrong: Documentary evidence is not to be summarized in the statement of claim. Documents will be dealt with through the affidavit of documents and discovery process, not in the pleadings.
why_D_wrong: A statement of claim is not accompanied by affidavit evidence. Affidavit evidence is used in motion proceedings. A statement of claim is a pleading, not a sworn document.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Statement of Claim — Required Contents
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.06(1)

tested_concepts: pleading material facts not evidence — r.25.06(1)
statement of claim required contents
facts vs evidence distinction in pleadings

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Statement of Claim — Time to Serve After Issue
fact_pattern: A plaintiff's lawyer issues a statement of claim on March 1, 2026. Due to difficulties locating the defendant, the plaintiff does not serve the claim until August 20, 2026 — which is 172 days after issuance. The defendant moves to have the claim dismissed as having been served out of time.
call_of_question: Is the statement of claim served out of time, and what is the applicable rule?

options.A: Yes — r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires a statement of claim to be served within 30 days of issuance; 172 days is far too late.
options.B: No — there is no deadline for serving a statement of claim after it is issued; the plaintiff can serve it at any time.
options.C: Yes — r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires a statement of claim to be served within 6 months of the date it was issued; service at 172 days is within this period so the motion should be dismissed.
options.D: Yes — the Rules require service within 90 days of issuance; 172 days exceeds this deadline.

explanation: Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a statement of claim shall be served within 6 months after the date it is issued. Six months from March 1, 2026 is September 1, 2026. Service on August 20, 2026 — which is 172 days after issuance — falls within the 6-month period and is therefore timely. The defendant's motion should be dismissed. If service had not been effected within 6 months, the plaintiff would need to obtain an order renewing the claim under r.14.08(2).
why_A_wrong: There is no 30-day service deadline for a statement of claim. Rule 14.08 provides a 6-month period for service after the date of issuance.
why_B_wrong: There is a service deadline: r.14.08 requires service within 6 months of issuance. A statement of claim cannot be served at any indefinite future time without renewal of the claim.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 14.08 requires service within 6 months of issuance. Service at 172 days (approximately 5.7 months) is within this period and is timely.
why_D_wrong: There is no 90-day service deadline for a statement of claim. The applicable period under r.14.08 is 6 months.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Statement of Claim — Time to Serve After Issue
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.08

tested_concepts: service deadline for statement of claim — 6 months from issuance
renewal of claim if not served in time

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Statement of Defence — Time to Deliver
fact_pattern: A defendant corporation was personally served with a statement of claim in Toronto on Monday, May 4, 2026. The defendant's counsel asks how many days the corporation has to deliver a statement of defence.
call_of_question: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how many days does a defendant served in Ontario have to deliver a statement of defence?

options.A: 10 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.B: 20 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.C: 30 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.D: 60 days after service of the statement of claim.

explanation: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been served with a statement of claim in Ontario must deliver a statement of defence within 20 days after service. Service occurred on May 4, 2026, so the defendant has until May 24, 2026 to deliver the statement of defence. If the defendant fails to deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time, the plaintiff may note the defendant in default under r.19.01.
why_A_wrong: The time to deliver a statement of defence for a defendant served in Ontario is 20 days, not 10 days. Ten days is too short.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 18.01 provides 20 days after service in Ontario to deliver a statement of defence.
why_C_wrong: The deadline for a defendant served in Ontario is 20 days, not 30 days. Forty days applies to defendants served outside Ontario (r.18.01(b)).
why_D_wrong: 60 days is not the applicable time period for a defendant served in Ontario. The correct period under r.18.01 is 20 days.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Statement of Defence — Time to Deliver
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.18.01

tested_concepts: time to deliver statement of defence — 20 days in Ontario
consequences of failing to defend

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Amendment of Pleadings — As of Right vs With Leave
fact_pattern: A plaintiff issued a statement of claim on February 10, 2026. The defendant delivered a statement of defence on March 12, 2026. The plaintiff has not yet replied to the defence and pleadings are not yet closed. On April 15, 2026, the plaintiff wishes to amend the statement of claim to add an additional defendant and a new cause of action.
call_of_question: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may the plaintiff amend the statement of claim without court leave at this stage?

options.A: No — a plaintiff may only amend a statement of claim with leave of the court once a statement of defence has been delivered.
options.B: Yes — under r.26.01, a party may amend a pleading once without leave at any time before the close of pleadings.
options.C: No — a plaintiff may never amend a statement of claim to add a new party; a separate action must be commenced.
options.D: Yes — but only if all parties consent to the amendment in writing.

explanation: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may amend a pleading once without leave before the close of pleadings. Pleadings are not yet closed here — the plaintiff has not yet replied to the statement of defence, so the pleadings remain open. The plaintiff may therefore amend the statement of claim once as of right, without obtaining court leave or the consent of other parties. After the close of pleadings, or after the right to amend as of right has been exercised, leave of the court is required under r.26.01.
why_A_wrong: Rule 26.01 permits one amendment as of right before the close of pleadings. Delivery of the statement of defence does not trigger a requirement for court leave; the right to amend as of right continues until pleadings close.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 26.01 permits one amendment as of right before the close of pleadings; no court leave or consent is required at this stage.
why_C_wrong: A plaintiff may add a new party by amendment. Adding parties by amendment to an existing proceeding is permitted; a separate action is not required as long as applicable limitations periods and procedural rules are satisfied.
why_D_wrong: Written consent of all parties is not required for an amendment as of right under r.26.01. The right to amend once without leave operates independently of the other parties' consent.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Amendment of Pleadings — As of Right vs With Leave
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01

tested_concepts: amendment as of right before close of pleadings — r.26.01
close of pleadings timing
when court leave is required to amend

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Amendment of Pleadings — Leave to Amend After Close of Pleadings
fact_pattern: The trial in a construction dispute is scheduled for six weeks' time. The plaintiff brings a motion for leave to amend the statement of claim to add a new head of damages that was not originally pleaded, relying on newly obtained expert evidence. The defendant argues that leave should be refused because the trial is imminent and the amendment would cause prejudice.
call_of_question: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the governing principle when a court considers whether to grant leave to amend a pleading, and how does prejudice factor into the analysis?

options.A: The court must refuse leave to amend once the trial has been scheduled; no amendments are permitted within 90 days of trial.
options.B: The court has an absolute discretion to grant or refuse leave to amend and applies no particular principle; each case is decided on its own unique facts.
options.C: Under r.26.01, leave to amend shall be granted on such terms as are just unless the amendment would result in prejudice to the other party that could not be compensated by costs.
options.D: The court will grant leave to amend only if the moving party demonstrates the proposed amendment raises a new cause of action that could not have been pleaded earlier.

explanation: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), on motion at any stage of an action, the court shall grant leave to amend a pleading on such terms as are just unless the amendment would result in prejudice to the other party that could not be compensated by costs. The governing principle favours granting leave to amend; the threshold is whether the opposing party would suffer non-compensable prejudice. Delay and the imminence of trial are factors that may bear on the prejudice analysis, but they do not automatically defeat an amendment motion. If the defendant's prejudice can be addressed by an adjournment and an award of costs thrown away, the amendment will generally be permitted.
why_A_wrong: There is no automatic rule prohibiting amendments within 90 days of trial. Rule 26.01 permits amendments at any stage on such terms as are just, with the key question being whether prejudice can be compensated by costs.
why_B_wrong: The court's discretion under r.26.01 is not unguided. The rule establishes that leave shall be granted unless the amendment causes non-compensable prejudice — there is a clear governing principle.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 26.01 requires the court to grant leave to amend on just terms unless the amendment would result in prejudice to the other party that cannot be compensated by costs.
why_D_wrong: There is no requirement that the proposed amendment raise a new cause of action or that it could not have been pleaded earlier. The relevant consideration is prejudice that cannot be compensated by costs.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Amendment of Pleadings — Leave to Amend After Close of Pleadings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01

tested_concepts: leave to amend — governed by prejudice compensable by costs
r.26.01 — shall grant leave unless non-compensable prejudice
amendment at any stage of action

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: The defendant in a fraud action receives the plaintiff's statement of claim but finds the allegations of fraud are stated in general terms without identifying specific representations, dates, or amounts alleged to have been fraudulently misrepresented. The defendant wants more detail before being required to plead.
call_of_question: What procedural step should the defendant take under the Rules of Civil Procedure to obtain more detailed information about the allegations?

options.A: The defendant should bring a motion to strike the statement of claim under r.25.11 because the pleading is deficient.
options.B: The defendant should demand particulars from the plaintiff under r.25.10; if the plaintiff fails to supply them, the defendant may move for a court order compelling particulars.
options.C: The defendant should simply file a statement of defence denying all allegations and seek particulars at the examination for discovery stage.
options.D: The defendant should bring a motion for summary judgment immediately on the grounds that the plaintiff's claim has no merit.

explanation: Under r.25.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may demand particulars of another party's pleading. A party who requires clarification of the details of another party's pleading may demand particulars in writing. If the other party fails to supply adequate particulars, the demanding party may bring a motion for a court order compelling production of particulars. Particulars are especially important in fraud allegations, which under r.25.06(8) must be pleaded with full particulars of the circumstances said to constitute fraud.
why_A_wrong: A motion to strike under r.25.11 is appropriate where the pleading is scandalous, frivolous, vexatious, or an abuse of process. A general pleading of fraud is not automatically grounds to strike — the appropriate remedy is to demand particulars under r.25.10.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The defendant should demand particulars under r.25.10, and if the plaintiff fails to comply, move for a court order compelling particulars.
why_C_wrong: While the defendant must eventually plead, waiting until discovery to seek particulars is inefficient and may waive the right to particulars before pleading. The Rules provide the specific mechanism of r.25.10 for this purpose.
why_D_wrong: A motion for summary judgment is not the appropriate remedy where the pleading lacks detail. Summary judgment addresses the merits of the claim; particulars address the adequacy of the pleading.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Particulars — Demand and Court Order
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.10; r.25.06(8)

tested_concepts: demand for particulars under r.25.10
court order for particulars
fraud must be pleaded with full particulars — r.25.06(8)

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Striking Out Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action (r.21.01)
fact_pattern: A defendant brings a motion under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure to strike the plaintiff's statement of claim on the grounds that it discloses no reasonable cause of action. The plaintiff's claim is novel and has not been recognised by Ontario courts, but is not clearly without foundation. No evidence is filed on the motion.
call_of_question: What is the test on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion to strike for no reasonable cause of action, and what is the outcome if the claim is novel?

options.A: The court will strike the claim unless the plaintiff proves on a balance of probabilities that the cause of action is well-established in Ontario law.
options.B: The court applies the test from Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc.: the claim will be struck only if it is plain and obvious, assuming all facts pleaded to be true, that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action; a novel but arguable claim should not be struck.
options.C: A motion under r.21.01(1)(b) must be supported by evidence; if no evidence is filed, the motion must be dismissed.
options.D: The court will strike any claim that has not been previously recognised by Ontario courts because such a claim has no reasonable prospect of success.

explanation: Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the applicable test is from Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959: the claim will be struck only if it is plain and obvious, assuming the facts as pleaded are true, that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action. The court assumes all facts pleaded to be true and gives the claim the most generous reading possible. No evidence is admissible on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion. A novel claim that raises an arguable point of law that has not been authoritatively settled should not be struck, as such claims deserve to be adjudicated at trial.
why_A_wrong: The plaintiff is not required to prove its case on a balance of probabilities on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion. All facts pleaded are assumed to be true; the only question is whether the pleading discloses a reasonable cause of action.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Hunt v. Carey test requires the claim be struck only if it is plain and obvious, on assumed facts, that no reasonable cause of action is disclosed. Novel claims survive the test.
why_C_wrong: No evidence is admissible on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion to strike. The motion is determined solely on the basis of the pleadings, with all facts assumed to be true.
why_D_wrong: Novelty alone is not grounds to strike. The Supreme Court in Hunt v. Carey confirmed that novel claims that raise arguable legal questions should not be struck — the law must be permitted to develop.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Striking Out Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.21.01(1)(b); Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959

tested_concepts: plain and obvious test — r.21.01(1)(b)
Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc. [1990] 2 SCR 959
no evidence on r.21.01 motion
novel claims survive r.21.01 motions

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Striking Out Pleadings — Scandalous, Frivolous, or Vexatious (r.25.11)
fact_pattern: A defendant's statement of defence contains a passage that makes personal attacks on the plaintiff's character, accuses the plaintiff's counsel of professional misconduct without any factual basis, and includes scandalous allegations irrelevant to any issue in the proceedings. The plaintiff brings a motion to strike those portions of the defence.
call_of_question: Under which rule may a court strike out portions of a pleading that are scandalous, frivolous, vexatious, or an abuse of process?

options.A: Rule 21.01(1)(b) — the court may strike the entire pleading if any portion discloses no reasonable cause of action.
options.B: Rule 25.11 — the court may strike out all or part of a pleading if it is scandalous, frivolous, vexatious, or may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial of the action.
options.C: Rule 26.01 — the court may require the party to amend the offending portions as a condition of proceeding.
options.D: Rule 37.14 — the court may set aside any order arising from improper pleadings on motion.

explanation: Under r.25.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may strike out or expunge all or part of a pleading (with or without leave to amend) on the grounds that it is scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious, or that it may prejudice or delay the fair trial of the action, or that it is otherwise an abuse of the process of the court. This rule addresses the content of pleadings — it targets material that should never have appeared in a pleading — as distinct from r.21.01, which addresses whether the pleading discloses a legally recognisable cause of action.
why_A_wrong: Rule 21.01(1)(b) addresses motions to strike an entire pleading for disclosing no reasonable cause of action. It does not target scandalous or irrelevant content within an otherwise valid pleading. Rule 25.11 is the appropriate rule.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 25.11 allows the court to strike out portions of a pleading that are scandalous, frivolous, vexatious, or would prejudice the fair trial of the action.
why_C_wrong: Rule 26.01 governs amendments to pleadings. While the court could order an amendment to cure a deficient pleading, the specific power to strike scandalous content is in r.25.11.
why_D_wrong: Rule 37.14 governs setting aside orders made by the court or a registrar. It does not address the striking of objectionable content from pleadings.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Striking Out Pleadings — Scandalous, Frivolous, or Vexatious
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.11

tested_concepts: striking scandalous or vexatious pleadings — r.25.11
distinction between r.25.11 and r.21.01
court power to strike portions of pleading

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Application vs. Action — When to Use Each
fact_pattern: A client believes her employer has wrongfully denied her contractual right to a share of a business sale. The dispute depends entirely on the proper legal interpretation of a written shareholders' agreement — there are no material facts in dispute. Counsel is deciding whether to proceed by way of action or application.
call_of_question: Under r.14.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which originating process is most appropriate where the relief sought requires the determination of a question of law or interpretation with no genuine factual dispute?

options.A: An action commenced by statement of claim is always the appropriate originating process; applications are only available for certain statutory proceedings.
options.B: An application under r.14.02 is the most appropriate process where the issues involve questions of law or interpretation on an agreed or undisputed factual record, without the need for trial or oral evidence.
options.C: A motion for summary judgment immediately on an action is the preferred approach where there are no material facts in dispute.
options.D: An application can only be used where all parties consent to the process; unilateral election of an application over an action is not permitted.

explanation: Under r.14.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a proceeding may be commenced by notice of application where it is authorized by statute or the Rules or where the relief claimed is a determination of rights under a document or statute, particularly where there are no material facts in dispute and the proceeding can be resolved on affidavit evidence without a trial. An application is heard by a judge on affidavit evidence and proceeds more quickly than an action through pleadings, discovery, and trial. Where the sole issue is the interpretation of a written contract (shareholders' agreement) on undisputed facts, the application is the more efficient and appropriate process.
why_A_wrong: Applications are not limited to statutory proceedings. Rule 14.02 permits applications where the relief sought is appropriate for determination on affidavit evidence, including interpretation of a document on undisputed facts.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 14.02 permits an application where there are no material facts in dispute and the proceeding turns on questions of law or interpretation; the matter is resolved on affidavit evidence before a judge.
why_C_wrong: While summary judgment is available, it requires commencing an action first. An application under r.14.02 is the more efficient originating process where there is no factual dispute and only legal interpretation is at issue.
why_D_wrong: An application does not require the consent of all parties. Any party may elect to proceed by application where the procedural requirements of r.14.02 are satisfied.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Application vs. Action — When to Use Each
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.02

tested_concepts: application vs action — r.14.02
application appropriate for legal questions on undisputed facts
affidavit evidence on application

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Litigation Guardians — Minors and Persons Under Disability
fact_pattern: A 14-year-old named Jonas is injured in a workplace accident and wishes to bring a personal injury action against his employer. His parents do not want to be involved. Counsel is retained to commence the action on Jonas's behalf.
call_of_question: Under r.7.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how must an action be commenced on behalf of a minor?

options.A: A minor may sue in his or her own name; no special procedural steps are required in Ontario civil litigation.
options.B: A minor must commence proceedings through a litigation guardian; the litigation guardian is named as a party and represents the minor's interests throughout the proceeding.
options.C: A minor must obtain a court order before commencing any civil proceeding, regardless of whether a litigation guardian is available.
options.D: A minor may only sue with the consent of both parents; if the parents do not consent, the action cannot be commenced until the minor turns 18.

explanation: Under r.7.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a proceeding may be commenced by or against a person under disability — including a minor — only by or through a litigation guardian. The litigation guardian acts on behalf of the minor and is named as a party representing the minor's interests. Where no willing family member is available, counsel may seek the appointment of the Office of the Children's Lawyer as litigation guardian or may seek court appointment of a guardian. The litigation guardian must consent in writing to act and must not have interests adverse to those of the minor.
why_A_wrong: A minor does not have the legal capacity to sue in their own name under the Rules. Rule 7.01 requires that a minor be represented by a litigation guardian throughout the proceeding.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 7.01 requires a litigation guardian for a minor; the guardian is named as a party and represents the minor's interests.
why_C_wrong: No prior court order is required simply to commence a proceeding on behalf of a minor. The litigation guardian may be designated without a court order; a court order is needed only in specific circumstances, such as approving a settlement.
why_D_wrong: The consent of both parents is not a prerequisite under r.7.01. If the parents are unwilling or unavailable, a litigation guardian may still be appointed by the court or the Office of the Children's Lawyer may act.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Litigation Guardians — Minors and Persons Under Disability
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.7.01(1)

tested_concepts: minor must sue through litigation guardian — r.7.01
litigation guardian named as party
Office of the Children's Lawyer

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Bellwood Construction Ltd. is a defendant in a negligence action brought by a homeowner for defective roofing work. Bellwood believes its subcontractor, Peak Roofing Inc., performed the defective work and is responsible for any damages. Bellwood wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Peak Roofing as part of the main action.
call_of_question: Under r.29.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a defendant commence a third-party claim, and must Bellwood obtain leave to do so?

options.A: A third-party claim may only be commenced with leave of the court in all circumstances; Bellwood must bring a motion for leave before issuing any third-party claim.
options.B: Under r.29.01, a defendant may issue a third-party claim without leave of the court if it is issued within the time for delivery of the statement of defence or before the close of pleadings; leave is required only if those deadlines have passed.
options.C: A third-party claim can only be issued if the main plaintiff consents to the addition of a third party to the action.
options.D: A defendant may never issue a third-party claim in a negligence action; contribution and indemnity must be claimed in a separate proceeding commenced after the main action is resolved.

explanation: Under r.29.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who claims contribution and indemnity from a person not already a party to the main action may issue a third-party claim. If the third-party claim is issued before the defendant's statement of defence or within the time for delivery of the statement of defence, no leave is required. If the defendant wishes to issue the third-party claim after the close of pleadings or at a later stage, leave of the court is required under r.29.01(2). The third-party claim is a separate pleading issued as part of the main action, enabling all related contribution and indemnity issues to be resolved in a single proceeding.
why_A_wrong: Leave is not required in all circumstances. Rule 29.01 permits the issuance of a third-party claim without leave if it is done within the time for delivery of the defence or before the close of pleadings.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.29.01, Bellwood may issue a third-party claim without leave if done within the required time; leave is required only after the close of pleadings.
why_C_wrong: The plaintiff's consent is not required for a defendant to issue a third-party claim. Rule 29.01 is a defendant's procedural right; it operates without the plaintiff's approval.
why_D_wrong: A defendant may claim contribution and indemnity from a third party within the main action by issuing a third-party claim under r.29.01. A separate subsequent action is not required and would be less efficient.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Third-Party Claims — Contribution and Indemnity (Rule 29)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.29.01

tested_concepts: third-party claim — r.29.01
no leave required if within time for defence
leave required after close of pleadings

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Statement of Claim — Form and Endorsement
fact_pattern: A lawyer is preparing a statement of claim for a commercial dispute. The client asks whether the statement of claim must include an endorsement and, if so, what the endorsement must contain.
call_of_question: Under r.14.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what must a statement of claim include as part of its form?

options.A: A statement of claim need not include an endorsement; the relief sought is set out only in the body of the claim.
options.B: A statement of claim must include a backsheet only, setting out the names of the parties and the court; no endorsement is required.
options.C: A statement of claim must be endorsed with a statement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought, which serves as a short-form summary of the action.
options.D: A statement of claim must include the complete evidence the plaintiff intends to rely upon at trial, endorsed on the backsheet.

explanation: Under r.14.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an originating process — including a statement of claim — shall be endorsed with a concise statement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought. This endorsement appears on the face of the claim and provides the defendant with a brief summary of what the action is about. The endorsement is distinct from the body of the claim, which sets out the full material facts and relief claimed in detail.
why_A_wrong: An endorsement is required under r.14.03. The statement of claim must include a concise endorsement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought.
why_B_wrong: A backsheet alone is insufficient. Rule 14.03 requires an endorsement of the nature of the claim and relief sought, not merely identification of the parties and court on a backsheet.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 14.03 requires the statement of claim to be endorsed with a concise statement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought.
why_D_wrong: Evidence is not to be included in the endorsement or anywhere in the statement of claim. Pleadings contain material facts, not evidence. An endorsement is a concise summary, not a complete evidence statement.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Statement of Claim — Form and Endorsement

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Notice of Action — Preserving Limitation Period Under r.14.08
fact_pattern: Osei & Partners LLP is retained to bring a product liability claim. The two-year limitation period will expire in ten days, but the statement of claim is not yet ready because the expert report is outstanding. Counsel considers whether there is a procedural mechanism to preserve the limitation period without immediately issuing a full statement of claim.
call_of_question: Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what document may a plaintiff issue to preserve the limitation period when the full statement of claim is not yet ready?

options.A: A plaintiff may file a caveat with the court to preserve the limitation period; no originating process needs to be issued.
options.B: Under r.14.08, a plaintiff may issue a notice of action, which preserves the limitation period; the plaintiff then has 30 days from the date the notice of action is issued to serve a statement of claim on the defendant.
options.C: A plaintiff may apply for an extension of the limitation period from the court; extensions are routinely granted where the statement of claim is not ready.
options.D: The plaintiff must issue the full statement of claim to preserve the limitation period; no abbreviated document is available to hold the limitation date.

explanation: Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a plaintiff is unable to draft a full statement of claim before the limitation period expires, the plaintiff may issue a notice of action. A notice of action preserves the limitation period from the date it is issued. The plaintiff then has 30 days from the date of issuance of the notice of action to serve a statement of claim on the defendant. If the statement of claim is not served within 30 days, the notice of action may expire and no longer protect the plaintiff's position. This mechanism is particularly useful where supporting expert evidence or other documentation needed to draft the claim is not yet available.
why_A_wrong: There is no mechanism for a 'caveat' to preserve a limitation period in Ontario civil procedure. The notice of action under r.14.08 is the proper document for this purpose.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 14.08 permits the plaintiff to issue a notice of action to preserve the limitation period, followed by service of the full statement of claim within 30 days.
why_C_wrong: Courts do not routinely grant extensions of limitation periods. The limitation period is a statutory deadline that generally must be met. The notice of action procedure under r.14.08 is the proper mechanism to address the situation described.
why_D_wrong: A full statement of claim is not the only option. Rule 14.08 specifically provides for a notice of action as an interim document to preserve the limitation period when the full claim is not ready.

source_reference.heading: Notice of Action — Preserving Limitation Period Under r.14.08
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.08

tested_concepts: notice of action — r.14.08 — preserves limitation period
30 days to serve statement of claim after notice of action
alternative to issuing full statement of claim

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Amendment of Pleadings — Leave Required After Close of Pleadings
fact_pattern: The plaintiff in a negligence action served a statement of claim and the defendant delivered a statement of defence. The plaintiff then discovers new information indicating that a second defendant should have been named. The plaintiff wishes to amend the statement of claim to add the new defendant after the close of pleadings. The defendant argues the amendment should be refused because the limitation period for the claim against the new defendant has now expired.
call_of_question: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, on what basis may the court grant or refuse leave to amend a statement of claim to add a new defendant after the close of pleadings, particularly where the limitation period may have expired?

options.A: Leave to amend is automatically granted once close of pleadings has occurred; there are no grounds on which the court may refuse the amendment.
options.B: Under r.26.01, the court shall grant leave to amend at any stage of an action on such terms as are just, unless the amendment would cause prejudice that could not be compensated by costs; the court has discretion to refuse an amendment adding a new defendant if the limitation period against that defendant has expired and adding them now would be prejudicial.
options.C: After close of pleadings, amendment is not available; the plaintiff must abandon the existing action and commence a new claim against the new defendant.
options.D: Leave to amend to add a new party is only available before trial; once close of pleadings has occurred, the amendment can only be made by consent of all existing parties.

explanation: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court shall grant leave to amend a pleading at any stage of an action on such terms as are just, unless prejudice would result that could not be compensated by costs or an adjournment. The right to amend is available after the close of pleadings and does not require consent of all parties. However, where the plaintiff seeks to add a new defendant and the limitation period against that defendant has expired, the court must consider whether the amendment would be prejudicial — adding a defendant against whom the limitation period has run is a significant consideration. The court will assess whether the new defendant knew about the potential claim, whether there is an arguable issue of discoverability, and whether the expiry of the limitation period against the new defendant is a bar to the amendment.
why_A_wrong: Leave to amend is not automatically granted. Rule 26.01 gives the court discretion to refuse an amendment that would cause prejudice that cannot be compensated by costs. The expiry of a limitation period is a significant factor against granting leave.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 26.01 permits amendment at any stage subject to the court's discretion; the expired limitation period against the new defendant is a key factor the court will weigh in deciding whether to grant leave.
why_C_wrong: Amendment is available after the close of pleadings under r.26.01; the plaintiff need not abandon the action. A new proceeding may face its own limitation problems. The amendment route under r.26.01 is the proper mechanism.
why_D_wrong: Consent of all existing parties is not required for an amendment under r.26.01. The court may grant leave on such terms as are just; the existing parties may oppose the amendment and the court weighs the arguments.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Amendment of Pleadings — Leave Required After Close of Pleadings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01

tested_concepts: r.26.01 — leave to amend at any stage
no prejudice that costs cannot compensate
expired limitation against new defendant — key factor

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Three defendants are named in a personal injury action. Defendant A — a property owner — believes that Defendant B — a snow removal contractor — is entirely responsible for the icy walkway that caused the plaintiff's injury, and wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Defendant B within the existing action.
call_of_question: Under r.28.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how may Defendant A assert its claim for contribution and indemnity against Defendant B within the main action?

options.A: Defendant A must commence a separate action against Defendant B; contribution and indemnity claims between co-defendants cannot be raised within the main action.
options.B: Defendant A may file a crossclaim against Defendant B within the main action under r.28.01, claiming contribution and indemnity or asserting that Defendant B alone is liable for the plaintiff's loss.
options.C: Defendant A may only claim contribution and indemnity from Defendant B by making a written demand to Defendant B's insurer; the claim cannot be made through any pleading in the main action.
options.D: Defendant A must wait until the plaintiff's main action is resolved before commencing a claim against Defendant B; cross-defendant claims cannot be brought while the main action is pending.

explanation: Under r.28.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant may crossclaim against any co-defendant — that is, another defendant who is already a party to the main action — by asserting a claim for contribution and indemnity or by claiming that the co-defendant alone is liable for the plaintiff's loss or a part of it. A crossclaim is a pleading filed within the main action; it enables all related claims among co-defendants to be resolved in a single proceeding without the need for a separate action. Defendant A should therefore file a crossclaim against Defendant B under r.28.01.
why_A_wrong: A separate action is not required. Rule 28.01 provides the mechanism for co-defendant claims within the main action. Commencing a separate action for contribution and indemnity would be procedurally unnecessary and less efficient.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 28.01 permits a defendant to file a crossclaim against a co-defendant for contribution and indemnity or for sole liability within the existing main action.
why_C_wrong: Contribution and indemnity is a legal claim that must be pursued through proper pleading procedures under the Rules. It is not resolved by writing to an insurer, and it may be raised as a crossclaim within the main action under r.28.01.
why_D_wrong: Defendant A need not wait until the main action is resolved. Rule 28.01 expressly permits the crossclaim to be made within the existing main action while that action is pending, enabling all parties and all related claims to be resolved together.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Crossclaims Between Co-Defendants — Rule 28
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.28.01

tested_concepts: crossclaim — r.28.01 — between co-defendants
claim contribution and indemnity or sole liability
within main action — no separate proceeding needed

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Time to Deliver Statement of Defence and Default
fact_pattern: A statement of claim for breach of contract is served on the defendant, Beaumont Logistics Inc., in Ontario on March 1. Beaumont's counsel asks how much time the company has to deliver its statement of defence.
call_of_question: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how many days does a defendant ordinarily have to deliver a statement of defence after being served with the statement of claim in Ontario?

options.A: 30 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.B: 20 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.C: 14 days after service of the statement of claim.
options.D: 60 days after service of the statement of claim.

explanation: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who is served with a statement of claim in Ontario must deliver a statement of defence within 20 days after service. If the defendant is served outside Ontario but within Canada or the United States, the time is 40 days; if served elsewhere, the time is 60 days. A defendant who does not deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time is noted in default, and the plaintiff may proceed to obtain default judgment under Part 19 of the Rules.
why_A_wrong: The time to deliver a statement of defence after service in Ontario is 20 days, not 30 days. Thirty days applies in different circumstances.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 18.01 provides 20 days to deliver a statement of defence after service within Ontario.
why_C_wrong: Fourteen days is not the prescribed period for delivering a statement of defence. The correct period for service in Ontario is 20 days under r.18.01.
why_D_wrong: Sixty days applies where the defendant is served outside Canada and the United States, not for service within Ontario. The correct period for Ontario service is 20 days under r.18.01.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Time to Deliver Statement of Defence and Default
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.18.01

tested_concepts: 20 days to deliver statement of defence — r.18.01
service in Ontario — 20 days
failure to defend — noted in default
